Entertainment.title: Bruno Mars brings hits, romance and a Paddington Bear to Wembley
Entertainment.text:
Bruno Mars appeared determined to lift the nation's mood during the first of his six nights at Wembley Stadium.

His last UK performance came in July 2018, shortly after England had been knocked out of the men's World Cup semi-finals. Eight years later, history had repeated itself.

But wearing an England football scarf, Union Jack bucket hat and clutching a Paddington Bear, the showman energised a 75,000-strong, multi-generational crowd.

After nearly a decade away from UK audiences, Mars proved why fans had snapped up tickets in record numbers, external.

The show began with a pre-recorded video of Mars praying in a church for a good performance, before he emerged beneath visuals of a stained-glass window in a red suit bejewelled with roses.

It was a suitably theatrical opening to a two-hour, 15-minute set. But for those who had avoided spoilers, Risk It All, a slow R&B ballad from Mars' latest album, The Romantic, was perhaps an unexpected opener.

Any surprise was short-lived. Within minutes, Mars sat behind a pair of bright red congas for Cha Cha, signalling the start of a set that moved between R&B, funk, Latin influences and pop.

It was an early glimpse of what was to come as he declared: "London, it's time to party."

The crowd quickly accepted the invitation. From then on, neither Mars nor his fans stood still.

24K Magic and Treasure prompted one of the night's biggest singalongs. As Mars sang "put your pinky rings up to the moon", thousands of little fingers rose into the air.

The opening run of songs showed why Mars has remained one of pop's most reliable live performers. Rather than relying on one sound, he moved between genres without ever losing the crowd.

Romance has always been at the heart of the singer's music, and it soon became the focus of the show.

"Tonight we sing about love," he told the crowd after Treasure, before introducing the Angel Baby Cam, a segment in which fans' reactions were projected onto the giant screens as he serenaded them.

It was one of several moments that played into his trademark romantic showmanship.

Later, he even emerged wearing a cape emblazoned with the words The Romantic, which appeared to nod to soul legend James Brown.

In the years between his solo releases, Mars has focused on collaborations, most notably Silk Sonic with Anderson .Paak, a partnership he has previously described as "natural and organic"., external

That chemistry was evident as Anderson .Paak joined Mars on stage for a four-song Silk Sonic segment, with the pair delivering tightly choreographed Motown-inspired dance routines and playful banter.

This included their attempts at British accents as they declared the evening to be "absolutely wicked".

Silk Sonic was not the only collaboration to feature. Although Mars has recently enjoyed chart success with Die With a Smile, alongside Lady Gaga, and APT. with Rosé, only the former made the setlist.

Performed during the show's ballad section, Die With a Smile prompted one of the evening's biggest singalongs, suggesting the song has earned its place alongside Mars' more established hits.

As the show drew to a close, Mars returned to the songs that helped cement his place as one of pop's biggest stars.

For his 2010 hit Just the Way You Are, the singer asked for the stadium lights to be turned up so he could see the crowd, revealing thousands of fans singing every word back to him, before Uptown Funk transformed Wembley into a giant dancefloor complete with fireworks and gold confetti.

The show ended with Dance With Me, a ballad in which Mars yearns for one last slow dance to save a struggling relationship.

Like Risk It All at the start of the evening, it was an unexpected choice to end the concert.

It left the crowd on a reflective note, perhaps hoping they would not have to wait another eight years to see him again.

His last UK performance came in July 2018, shortly after England had been knocked out of the men's World Cup semi-finals. Eight years later, history had repeated itself. But wearing an England football...

Entertainment.title: Gracie Abrams' Daughter from Hell: Rebellion never sounded so toothless
Entertainment.text:
Knives. So many knives. Gracie Abrams' third album is like a cutlery drawer full of daggers.

They're stuck in her back, waiting to be twisted by a two-faced friend. She's "cut to the bone" by the careless words of a lover. Abrams even has a song called The Knife, where she proclaims she'll live with a blade "in my side", as an act of defiance.

"They're daring me to pull it out / I'll probably keep it for a lifetime."

Her lyrics are equally sharp and penetrating. The album has a broad theme of responsibility, as Abrams accepts blame for her mistakes, without letting others off the hook.

On the stunning ballad Good Reason, she struggles with the idea of a relationship fizzling out, with no major disagreements, just the gut feeling it's not working.

"I'm only half sure that I mean it," she sighs as she calls it off. "If only I had a good reason."

Broke My Heart finds her on the other side of a break-up. "How could I know you and not have a clue?" she pleads. "No difference to you / but you just broke my heart."

The knife, she's realised, cuts both ways.

Abrams' record is called Daughter From Hell, a reference to her rebellious teenage years, and it arrives to a flurry of anticipation.

The 26-year-old has been making introspective, confessional pop since the end of the 2010s, but her career really took off with the release of her debut album, Good Riddance in 2023. That year, she supported Taylor Swift on the Eras tour, and won a Grammy nomination for best new artist.

She earned her first UK number one in 2024 with That's So True, a single from the deluxe edition of her second album, The Secret Of Us. Anticipation for her new work has been building since last year, when she performed two new songs – Death Wish and Cold Goodbyes – on tour.

They hinted at a darker, more gothic aesthetic, with lyrics haunted by ghostly figures and existential crises.

Cold Goodbyes, in particular, is set to the sound of an unsettling synth drone – a world away from the gently-plucked ballads of her earlier work.

Sadly, it's an outlier. The music on Daughter From Hell is largely the same as before: low key, whisper-soft, self-interrogating, floaty and inconsequential.

The pianos are always muted, the drums all sound like they're being played next door, and the orchestra is buried deep in the mix to foreground Abrams' voice.

That's not a consistently bad choice. When Abrams sings about feeling like a burden on the lead single Hit The Wall, for example, her vocals are isolated in a way that emphasises her loneliness and disconnection.

But over the album's 16 tracks, the gossamer production – by indie-folk guru Aaron Dessner (Taylor Swift, Ed Sheeran) – is too insubstantial to sustain interest.

Worse still, it's at odds with the serrated edges of Abrams' lyrics.

Take the song Humming, which observes the US through the eyes of someone who's grown up knowing nothing but economic turmoil and the politics of hostility.

"Every kid I grew up with has lost their childhood house," sings Abrams. "And there's no one at the top to believe / What a way to feel in your 20s."

The power of that writing is let down by another plodding arrangement, stripping Abrams' words of their urgency.

When she sings "Let me wake up from this horrible dream", it sounds like she's sleepwalking.

Thankfully, there are a few moments when the album sputters into life.

She's more candid on Look At My Life – spilling secrets about the music industry and her relationship with fame over a strobing synth line.

"I got what I wanted and it doesn't sit right," she observes, describing parties full of "big shots and barbies" whose empty talk makes her "ears bleed".

Co-produced with Olivia Rodrigo's chief collaborator Dan Nigro, it's one of the few songs with an elevated pulse rate.

The other is Mini Bar, a breathless account of nights out, laced with bad decisions and social anxiety.

That one's a co-write with her best friend, the pop singer Audrey Hobert – which makes you wonder what Abrams could do if she broke away from Dessner's muted production style.

It's frustrating because there are a handful of excellent songs here (Hit The Wall, Look At My Life, Death Wish, Good Reason), and Abrams generally does a superb job of capturing the restless uncertainty and of early adulthood.

If only she could deliver the songs with the conviction of her lyrics, she'd have a classic on her hands.

What she needs is a good knife sharpener.

She's "cut to the bone" by the careless words of a lover. Abrams even has a song called The Knife, where she proclaims she'll live with a blade "in my...

Entertainment.title: Judge orders pause on Paramount-Warner merger after challenge from 12 states
Entertainment.text:
A federal judge on Monday ordered Paramount and Warner Bros Discovery to halt their $81bn merger for at least two weeks, allowing states that are challenging the deal more time to see their case through in court.

Twelve states, led by California, sued to block Paramount’s pending buyout of Warner last week – alleging that such a combination would “extinguish competition” in Hollywood and lead to fewer choices for consumers, particularly moviegoers and cable customers across the US.

The states’ top prosecutors called on Warner and Paramount to not close the transaction until after a court had time to “fully evaluate” their claims. And when the companies refused, they filed for a temporary restraining order – which is what district judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín granted on Monday. That opens the door to a potential preliminary injunction that the states are also seeking to effectively block the deal.

“This is a critical first win in our case to ensure this megamerger never sees the light of day,” Rob Bonta, the California attorney general, said in a statement following Monday’s order. “History tells the tale of what happens when a few people have great power over markets that are central to Americans’ lives: fewer opportunities for more people, worse products and services for all people.”

A Warner-Paramount tie-up would bring together two of the five last legacy studios in Hollywood – as well as host of TV networks, titles filling streaming libraries and news operations. That would include Warner’s HBO Max, fan favorites such as Harry Potter and even CNN coming under the same roof of Paramount-owned CBS, movies including Top Gun and the Paramount+ streaming service.

Paramount did not immediately comment on Monday’s order. But the company, which was bought out by Skydance just last year, has vowed to “vigorously defend” its Warner acquisition. Paramount previously called the states’ complaint “wrong on both the facts and the law”, maintaining that a merger would instead strengthen competition against bigger entertainment rivals. And it touted regulatory greenlights the deal has received elsewhere, including from the Trump administration last month.

The temporary restraining order granted on Monday halts the deal from progressing for at least 14 days, although the pause could be extended for up to 28 days. The court has set 3 August as a date for a hearing on the states’ preliminary injunction motion, although that schedule could also be pushed back.

Entertainment.title: ‘The Kardashian era is over’: TikTok’s ‘povvo king’ takes on the billionaires
Entertainment.text:
It was ice cubes that tipped Shabaz Ali over the edge. “A woman online spent her entire day making content about ‘designer ice cubes’,” he recalls. “She’d freeze flavoured waters or fruit juices and show the whole process in tedious detail. I was like, ‘There’s no way she’s got a proper job or has ever struggled for money. Only someone wealthy would have the time to do this.’ It vexed me, so on a whim, I made a reaction video saying ‘On today’s episode of I’m Rich, You’re Poor …’ Soon my TikToks were getting 30m views and thousands of comments. It blew my mind.”

Ali, AKA @ShabazSays, has since become a cult celebrity for his sarcastic commentary on how the other half live. His scathing takedowns of the “rich and ridiculous” have won him more than 4 million followers. Now he’s transferring his viral fame to our TV screens.

It all began when former chemistry teacher Ali, 32, took to his bedroom in Blackburn during lockdown and amused himself by mocking the smugly privileged. In his crosshairs were those who humblebrag about their perfect lives and woo-woo habits. Tongue firmly in cheek, he styled himself as “King of the Povvos” and leader of the “#PovvoGang”.

“It was reclaiming an insult,” he says. “I grew up working class and heard ‘povvo’ so much. The community we’ve built is warm, honest and self-deprecating. They’re saying, ‘We don’t have much but at least we’re here’. Povvo is a badge of honour now.”

The disconnect between influencers and their audience is increasingly jarring. “They post about bespoke Bentleys and private jets. Who are you making this for? [US socialite] Becca Bloom really bothers me. Millions of people watch her cats eating out of £20,000 bowls. She has a £100k Louis Vuitton trunk full of Hermès bags. Yet her followers genuinely think they’re friends.”

In Ali’s corner of the internet, a backlash was brewing: “People were tagging me in dozens of videos per day, pointing out tone-deaf displays of wealth.” He calls it “a movement” that is helping to move the dial on unrealistic standards. “There’s been a massive shift in the last few years,” says Ali. “The Kardashian era is over. We don’t want airbrushed reality any more. We want it raw, authentic and unfiltered.”

In these days of doomscrolling, aspirational figures fill a void. “As a teacher, I saw how social media warped teenagers’ perception of the world. Wealth influencers, trad wives and Andrew Tate provide something that’s missing from their lives. The illusion of working hard and getting your reward has been shattered. They’re seeing their parents and teachers struggle. Young people dream of opulence but can’t have it. They’d say, ‘I don’t need to study, I’m going into business. Richard Branson didn’t do his GCSEs.’ I was like, ‘He’s one person!’ There are 3,400 billionaires in a global population of 8.3 billion. That’s a lot of disappointed people.”

Amid the cost-of-living crisis, his videos provided a reality check. “I was telling my students, ‘This lavish stuff online isn’t right. It’s not real’. That’s why I started taking the mickey. It snowballed.”

His BBC documentary, Shabaz Asks: Are Billionaires Bad?, finds Ali investigating extreme wealth and its real-world impact. With his trademark playful wit, he asks whether the mega-rich are a symptom of a broken culture or if they can be a force for good.

Wealth hoarding gives him “the ick”. “What annoys me most is that the gap is widening,” he says. “We’re getting more billionaires, who are finding more tax loopholes and ways to hoard assets. I genuinely think 90% of them don’t care. You can’t be a billionaire and be a good person.”

In a bid to understand more, he attempts to meet a billionaire in person. Ali approaches the Kardashians, Beckhams, Trumps, Elon Musk, Jeff Bezos and Beyoncé for an interview. “Kim K definitely saw my message. She didn’t reply, though. A couple of people hearted it. That was it.”

Instead he meets a range of people who are both pro and anti-billionaire. These include the Green party’s Zack Polanski. “He’s the only person on the party political spectrum who’s speaking out about billionaires,” says Ali. “Everybody else is too busy trying to protect their donors. Many of his arguments resonated. I’m a lifelong Labour voter but might I vote Green now? Possibly.”

He spends time with Apprentice winner turned “eight-figure entrepreneur” Joseph Valente (“fascinating character but he uses a lot of hairspray”). He flies to the Colorado ski resort of Vail to stay with Phones 4U founder John Caudwell. “I hate billionaires but I liked John, which was hard,” admits Ali. “I just think he’s unaware of his own power and privilege. He told me, ‘You can’t whisper in the ears of governments. I had a meeting with Keir Starmer and he didn’t take my advice.’ The fact that he even said that sentence! You got into that room because of money. The prime minister might not have taken your advice but there’s someone whose advice he did take. I admire John’s philanthropy but fundamentally we still disagree. Your rich friends are still wankers, mate!”

Ali takes the opportunity to poke around inside Caudwell’s kitchen, hoping to find one of the vast “curated” fridges he satirises online. “I genuinely thought I was going to get the internet lifestyle with everything neat and labelled but it was like a normal person’s fridge. It was dirtier than mine!” Did making the film challenge Ali’s views? “It didn’t alter my beliefs, it cemented them. I still think we don’t need billionaires and they should be doing more for the rest of us.”

He’s told that if we make billionaires pay more tax, they will leave the UK and take their wealth with him. “Let them go!” he says. “Let’s look over the hill and see if the grass is greener. It’s just scare tactics. They ain’t going anywhere. They’ve benefited too much from this country and have too much of a stake here to suddenly leave. Tax them, I say. Tax diamonds. Tax yachts.” He laughs. “If they don’t pay up, move them all to the Isle of Wight and turn it into a giant prison for rich people.”

This future star’s debut documentary ends with a shameless appeal for a full series. “We’ve already started talking about the next film,” he says. “Skincare, cosmetics and plastic surgery is the route I’m keen to go down. Shabaz asks: can I turn back time? That’s the question I want to pose. Watch this space.”

In the meantime, he believes society is approaching tipping point. “I think we’re on the brink of something big,” says Ali with a twinkle of zeal in his eyes. “People are so sick of wealth inequality. We’re French Revolution era, ready to overthrow Marie Antoinette.”
Entertainment.description: It was ice cubes that tipped Shabaz Ali over the edge. “A woman online spent her entire day making content about ‘designer ice cubes’,” he recalls. “She’d freeze flavoured waters or fruit juices and show the whole process in tedious detail. I was like, ‘There’s no way she’s got a proper job or has ever struggled...

Entertainment.title: ‘His chords are like sheets of diamond ice’: the beguiling psych-folk of late cult songwriter Ed Askew
Entertainment.text:
In a grainy television clip from 1986, a flame-haired and neckerchiefed troubadour plucks away at a lute-like instrument, harmonica under chin, the gentle ripples giving way to a voice full of longing. This performance, uploaded to YouTube in 2010, has generated only a handful of comments, but peruse any of them (“Ed the Great!”; “pure gold”) and you begin to understand the passion that Ed Askew – an under-the-radar US poet, artist and musician – has inspired over the years.

“He was living in a world that was for people who didn’t fit in,” Askew’s collaborator, Jerry David DeCicca, says on the free-spiritedness of his friend, whose musical life began with discovering the 10-stringed tiple as a teenager in the 1950s and ended with his death a year ago, aged 84, on the verge of him releasing his album The Final Painting, now being released posthumously this month.

Its producer, DeCicca, first fell for what he calls Askew’s “singularity” in the early 00s. But in recent years, he has captured the hearts of many more of music’s most characterful contemporary acts: from Sharon Van Etten and Bill Callahan, both of whom lend their vocals to The Final Painting, to Glasgow psych-folk band Trembling Bells who brought Askew to London’s Cafe Oto in 2017, and indie-Americana singer-songwriter Steve Gunn, who accompanied him at gigs in the early 2010s. “I got to tour with him in 2011 and felt his Jesus-y vibes,” Callahan says. “Jesus with a little scamp mixed in.” For Callahan, there is a “primordial” element in Askew’s music, that “makes it feel like the world is evolving right before your eyes when his song is playing. There’s a holy aspect to it – those piano chords constructed like sheets of diamond ice.”

From the scent of a marigold to the death of a Kennedy, what stands out the most about Askew’s songwriting is his flâneur-like observations, evoking the quotidian and the tremendous. In 2011, Askew cited his early influences as Kurt Weill, John Cage and Erik Satie, while others have tried to compare Askew to the likes of Robert Wyatt, Gaslight-era Bob Dylan or the Incredible String Band. The truth is that his baroque songwriting bears more resemblance to the multiple-angled paintings of Cézanne – an artist he greatly admired – or the writing of Joe Brainard mixed with the poetry of Lorca, than it does any of its musical equivalents.

Askew’s song Moon in the Mind appeared on the soundtrack of Netflix’s smash-hit series Baby Reindeer in 2024, an inclusion that was welcome and unexpected in the last year of his life, DeCicca says. But for the most part, Askew wasn’t concerned with acclaim or commerciality. “He just wasn’t wired that way,” DeCicca says; instead, Askew simply focused on writing his fine-spun songs on a day-to-day basis.

He displayed that beatnik essence from the very beginning. Born in Connecticut in 1940, Askew studied painting at Yale School of Art in the early 1960s, before moving to New York for a brief spell, playing for tips in the “basket house” folk music venues of the East Village in 1967. It was here that he attracted the attention of New York-based record company ESP-Disk – usually more inclined towards free jazz giants such as Sun Ra and Pharoah Sanders – who put out his 1968 self-titled debut (later retitled Ask the Unicorn), but soon shelved his follow-up Little Eyes, which wasn’t released until 2002.

Over the course of his life, Askew put out approximately 50 albums between labels, archival releases, live albums and self-releases, “with more still unreleased in his vault”, says DeCicca. This was mostly a home-recorded and self-published enterprise funded by house painting and art teaching, plus welfare assistance for a time – before he returned to the studio in 2011. Regarding a song on the new record titled Someone, with Callahan on vocals, DeCicca wonders if Askew is waiting in a social security office while they’re calling out numbers: “16”, “22” and “46” are delivered by Callahan with his usual deadpan delivery. “I think if most people wrote a song like that it would be about their agitation but Ed’s point of view is so different,” DeCicca says. But who knows if it’s really about social security at all, such is the impressionistic nature of Askew’s lyric writing.

Jay Pluck, Askew’s close friend and collaborator, points out just how pioneering Askew’s psych-folk songwriting was in the 1960s. “It was quite brave for him to be writing openly gay love songs so early on,” he tells me. And yet: “Over the years, I noticed that no one really knew that he was gay and I don’t know why – there are gay love songs on Ask the Unicorn and Little Eyes.” Pluck mentions a later song called Lamar, recorded on cassette tape in the late-90s, in which Askew is “professing his love in a Greek paean kind of way”.

Would Askew have described himself as a gay songwriter, I wonder? “I think he would,” Pluck answers – before pausing. “Although he could be a contrarian. If you asked him if he thought he was a great gay artist, he’d say no. But if you left off ‘gay’ he’d be like, why?” Pluck laughs. For Askew, his sexuality was simply who he was, as much a part of his songs as anything else.

Yet for Askew, “like most obscure musicians, there was never that Searching for Sugar Man blast,” DeCicca says, citing the 2012 documentary that launched Mexican-American musician Rodríguez out of obscurity and into late-in-life fame. “What allowed Ed to gradually find a global audience was not one event shining a spotlight; it was that he lived, day in and day out, as a true artist, sharing meaningful breadcrumbs for more and more people to discover.”

It’s a slow-burn tale in the age of the algorithm, and a fitting one for a musician who lived life on his own terms: “I play music for you to feel,” he once sang.

“There is no trying in his music,” Callahan says. “It just is.”

This performance, uploaded to YouTube in 2010, has generated only a handful of comments, but peruse any of them...

Entertainment.title: The Odyssey: jaw-dropping action, dark magic and dolphin cameos – discuss with spoilers
Entertainment.text:
The Odyssey looks set to be the blockbuster hit of the summer, with global opening weekend grosses of $264m, critical rave reviews including five stars from the Guardian’s Peter Bradshaw, and reports of fans traversing continents to experience Christopher Nolan’s epic tale of gods and monsters in one of the few dozen screens worldwide with the capability to screen Imax in 70mm. It also has the potential to rule the box office for the coming weeks, with few other true blockbusters to be released this summer, with the exception of Spider-Man: Brand New Day (hence the cast’s humiliation ritual of a press tour). Read on for a spoiler-packed breakdown of the epic battles, narrative twists and out-of-nowhere cameos that bring richness to Nolan’s idiosyncratic vision of ancient life.

A monumental spectacle

After Oppenheimer won best picture at the 2024 Oscars, Nolan knew he had a once-in-a-lifetime chance to realize a decades-long dream of bringing Homer to the big screen. “OK, I’ve now got an opportunity to make a film I wouldn’t be able to make otherwise,” he recently told the Guardian. You can see every penny of The Odyssey’s reported $250m budget on screen. The landscapes of Ithaca are breathtakingly beautiful, and there’s a creepy pall to our hero’s journey to the underworld that Nolan achieved by shooting on the black sands of Iceland’s beaches under the hazy light of the midnight sun. (Fun fact: Nolan also used Icelandic glaciers as the backdrop for the alien planet of Interstellar.)

Nolan filmed in six countries to achieve the range of landscapes in the film, which also included Scotland, Greece, Italy and Malta, and, more controversially, Western Sahara. Controlled by Morocco, the region is often referred to as Africa’s last colony, with the UN classifying it as a non-decolonized territory. “Audiences coming to see The Odyssey deserve better,” the artist and writer Mohamed Sleiman Labat wrote in an essay for the Guardian. “Those cinematic shots sold to them as the places and moments where historical epics took place were captured at the expense of the Sahrawi people’s suffering.”

The film’s epic scenes in the Mediterranean were filmed in the ocean, with Nolan eschewing CGI in favor of real roiling seas and Odysseus and his crew aboard the only functional Viking ship in the world (the delightfully named Draken Harald Hårfagre). A replica of the boat was built for scenes shot in a colossal water tank on the Universal lot. “There were these two jet engines, like 737 engines, blowing water on us,” Damon has said. “It was definitely about as hard as you can make water work in a controlled environment.”

Technical wizardry

Nolan has been a champion of Imax for two decades: in 2008, The Dark Knight was the first major Hollywood film to use the cameras for its action sequences. The camera set-up has been used increasingly prominently in his films since then. Nolan used half a dozen Imax cameras to shoot the film, all enclosed in soundproof “blimp” casing to mask their loud operational whirr.

The team also worked with an LED company to create lighting rigs they called “pyrohedrons” (fire pyramids) to give the illusion that warm firelight was casting a glow over interior and night-time scenes. “I wanted the camera to see what the human eye would see,” Nolan told the New York Times.

Bringing the divine down to earth

In Homer’s epic poem, gods are “self-interested beings, whose interventions in human lives are motivated primarily by their own desires, whims, and preferences”, as classicist Emily Wilson put it in her translation. Yet Nolan’s vision of how gods, demi-gods and mortals interact is somewhat earthier than in Homer, with Zendaya’s Athena less of a fearsome divine force than a pal and a confidante to Odysseus.

Charlize Theron is always a treat (even in her ropey Netflix fare), but Nolan’s interpretation of nymph Calypso may not feel strong enough for some viewers to work as a framing device for the bulk of the film. In Homer, the nymph is a cruel enslaver who holds Odysseus captive on her island; Nolan sees the character as lonely and somewhat pitiable. It’s worth wondering what Theron would have done with a spikier version of the role – she excels as a cartoon villain.

Like classicist Emily Hauser, who recently wrote for the Guardian about Nolan’s omissions, some viewers may also miss the Homeric princess Nausicaa. While interacting with Odysseus, she sniffily pooh-poohs him as “an unpromising man” – her barbs would have added a little light relief to the picture.

When Lupita Nyong’o was announced as Helen of Troy – the face that launched a thousand ships – rightwing commentators denounced the casting choice for predictably small-minded reasons. It is ironic that the character is afforded little screen time: when Tom Holland’s Telemachus goes to visit Helen and her husband, Menelaus (Jon Bernthal) in Sparta midway through the film, the famed beauty is near mute, with a grisly scar covering the left side of her face. It’s a bold choice from Nolan, but a little odd that we never find out the cause of her injury.

Odysseus’s loyal wife Penelope (Anne Hathaway) is a steely and cunning presence despite the suitors’ invasion of her palace. “We wanted to make sure that she did not look like she was mourning her husband,” said costume designer Ellen Mirojnick, who dressed Hathaway in the red and blue colors of Ithaca. “She was always viewed as the queen.” The actor has likened her character to a slowly simmering volcano that eventually erupts in scenes where she curses the suitors and pushes her double-crossing servant Melantho (Mia Goth) to certain death while the palace is at war.

One of the film’s most memorable scenes sees Odysseus and his men face the sirens, whose alluring song can lure sailors to their death. As their boat approaches their island, Odysseus is tethered to a mast to hear their beautiful yet terrible song while it is muffled to the audience’s ears, as if we, like Odysseus’s first mate Eurylochus (Himesh Patel), have our ears plugged with wax. The sound is “all the things you want it to be, then all the things you wish you never wished for”, says Odysseus, as Nolan powerfully leaves it to the audience’s imagination to fill in the blank.

Some of the film’s most touching moments come from John Leguizamo, barely recognisable with white hair and a dirty smock in the role of Eumaeus, the nearly-blind swineherd that remains loyal to Odysseus to the very end. As Greek military leader Agamemnon, Benny Safdie strikes a fearsome presence in a smart bit of against-type casting from Nolan. Meanwhile, not all cameos were planned: a pod of dolphins who made a surprise appearance on a day of shooting ended up making the film’s final cut.

Yet perhaps most surprising of all is the casting of rapper Travis Scott as a bard storyteller who opens the movie with a story of Odysseus’s heroic exploits at Troy. As he orates, the character pounds his staff on the floor in the tempo of dactylic hexameter (which as every good classicist knows, is the original meter of Homer’s poem). “I cast him because I wanted to nod towards the idea that this story has been handed down as oral poetry, which is analogous to rap,” Nolan said in an interview with Time.

It’s likely far too early to make many accurate predictions about next year’s Oscars, given that many of the awardsiest-seeming films won’t premiere until fall film festivals. But Nolan is one of the Academy’s favorite directors, winning seven out of Oppenheimer’s 13 nominations in 2024 and a respectable three out of eight for Dunkirk a few years prior. The Odyssey seems like a lock for nominations in best picture, best director and most of the craft categories, leaving the acting categories as a toss-up.

There’s no debate about who the male lead of the film is, but there’s all to play for in the supporting categories, with Holland and Robert Pattinson eligible for supporting actor and Zendaya, Nyong’o and Theron potential competitors in the supporting actress category – and Hathaway, if Universal decides not to run her in lead. But I’d personally lean towards Leguizamo for his turn as Eumaeus, and Samantha Morton’s staggering, scene-stealing role as Circe, a witch who turns Odysseus’s men into pigs.

Being something of an outsider to the Hollywood star system hasn’t stopped Morton getting two Oscar nominations to date (for Sweet and Lowdown and In America). With the weight of Nolan’s film behind her, I’ll be gunning for her to go all the way this time.

Examining the true cost of war

There’s a good case to be made that The Odyssey’s best sequence is its final 30 minutes, where Odysseus finally makes it back home with the help of Athena and sneaks into his Ithaca palace disguised as a beggar man. After Penelope challenges the men to string his bow – and Odysseus is the only one able to – he throws off his rags and reveals his true identity.

His face-off with the suitors isn’t the merciless slaughter that Homer described – it’s a fair fight with both sides armed – but the suitors, led by Pattinson’s slimy Antinous, are no match for the master of the house. His recapturing of Ithaca is bittersweet, interspersed with flashbacks to the “fires of anarchy and pain” that blazed through Troy as the Greeks sacked the city. There’s no glory in war for Nolan, echoing the sentiments of Dunkirk and Oppenheimer. As Odysseus puts it: “We have broken the fragile bond between men.”

BreakingNewsTab.title: Whitehall officials offered move to No 10 North in Manchester
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Civil servantsworking for government departmentsbased in London have been offered relocation packages to encourage them to move to the North of England, the BBC understands.

It comes as the first details are emerging about the new No 10 North which has already started work, according to sources close to new Prime Minister Andy Burnham.

The BBC has been told that the Northern branch of Downing Street will be overseen by cabinet minister and ally of Burnham, Louise Haigh.

Haigh, who is the first secretary of state, is thought to have been asked to lead a new Office for the Prime Minister and the Cabinet (OPMC), which will give her control of key decision- making bodies across government.

The OPMC will include No 10, No 10 North and teams within the Cabinet Office.

Announcing the changes,, external which will also include an AI Taskforce, Leader of the House of Lords, Baroness Angela Smith said No 10 North would be a "dominant driver of the UK's economy".

It is understood that the initial location of No 10 North will be Heron House in central Manchester, in the same building which houses offices for the intelligence service GCHQ. Specialist police officers have been seen guarding the building.

It is likely that the longer term base for No 10 North will be at a new government "Digital Campus" which is under construction in the Ancoats area of Manchester. It is not due for completion until 2032.

However, a good indication of what it could look like comes from a new report, released on Tuesday.

It comes as the first details are emerging about the new No 10 North which has already started work, according to sources close to new Prime...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Ann Widdecombe was hit on head repeatedly with hammer, prosecutors say
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Ann Widdecombe died after being hit on the head with a hammer 21 times at her Devon home as she ate lunch, prosecutors have told Westminster Magistrates' Court.

Prosecutor Kashif Malik said the man accused of her murder, Joshua Kerry, arrived at her house on July 8 in a red Vauxhall Corsa and entered through the front door.

Kerry, who prosecutors say was only in the property for two minutes and was wearing black gloves, then allegedly hit Widdecombe repeatedly with a hammer, tipped her from her chair and took her wallet before driving off, the court heard.

The 28-year-old from South Yorkshire, who is charged with murder, was remanded in custody to appear at the Old Bailey later on Tuesday.

Malik told the court that Widdecombe did not attend a pre-arranged online interview with Channel 5's Matt Allwright show that had been scheduled for just before 13:00 BST on 8 July.

The show's production team tried to contact Widdecombe through her personal assistant, who then asked Widdecombe's gardener to check on her, the court heard.

Prosecutors said the gardener entered the house via the open front door on 9 July, and found Widdecombe lying face down on her kitchen floor with a serious head injury.

Malik told the chief magistrate that security camera footage from her home in Haytor showed she had been fatally attacked the day before.

He added that police were still investigating whether there was a terrorism connection or political motivation for the attack.

Kerry, from Rotherham, appeared emotionless in the dock and spoke only to confirm his name, date of birth and address during the short hearing at Westminster Magistrates' Court. He did not indicate a plea.

Kerry was charged with one count of murder last night following an investigation led by counter-terrorism detectives.

The court heard that a pathologist had given a provisional cause of Widdecombe's death as a blunt force injury to the head.

But earlier on Tuesday, the opening of an inquest into her death heard that while a post-mortem examination had taken place, "at this stage the precise medical cause of death has yet to be established".

BreakingNewsTab.title: Row escalates in Ukraine over army chief's future
BreakingNewsTab.text:
As discussions continue to resolve the crisis caused by President Zelensky's decision last week to part ways with Ukraine's popular Defence Minister, Mykhailo Fedorov, those organising daily protests in support of the minister have issued a threat.

"If, by Friday July 24th [commander-in-chief Oleksandr] Syrskyi has not been dismissed and Federov has not been nominated for the post of Minister of Defence," army veteran Dmytro Koziatynskyi wrote on social media, "we will begin an open-ended nationwide protest."

No protests are currently scheduled before then. Ukraine is waiting, anxiously, for a resolution.

Over the weekend, the news was full of reports of meetings and discussions, as Zelensky consulted ministers and generals.

Amid a welter of rumours, the General Staff was forced to deny reports that Gen Syrskyi was about to be – or even had already been – sacked. Another report suggested that Fedorov had refused an offer to return to a different position in government.

Kyrylo Budanov, head of the president's office, urged Ukrainians to remain calm.

"The situation surrounding the latest internal changes has caused strong emotions," he wrote on Tuesday.

With the noisy calls of angry protesters echoing around the tight confines of Ivana Franka Square, within earshot of the presidential palace, Budanov said the government was taking note.

"I understand people who express their position…..The position of society has been heard."

With large numbers of demonstrators directing their anger towards Gen Syrskyi – seen by many as representing outdated Soviet-era military doctrines, Budanov, a former head of military intelligence, warned people "not to hate anyone".

"The enemy is closely monitoring our every internal dispute and is waiting for us to start fighting among ourselves," he wrote.

"The work is under way. There will be results."

On Monday, the beleaguered but usually silent 60-year-old Gen Syrskyi hit back against his critics, publishing a long defence of his record and urging readers not to focus on his widely-reported rivalry with 35-year-old Mykhailo Fedorov.

Despite Zelensky's claim last week that the two men could not even bear to sit in the same room, Syrskyi said it had been a surprise to learn that he had a conflict with the minister.

"I perceived our relationship as a working one," he wrote in an op-ed for the Militarny website.

"If I offended anyone, Mykhailo Albertovych [Fedorov], I'm sorry."

The general said the issues of military contracts, procurement and mobilisation currently being aired in the press and boiled down to pithy slogans on pieces of cardboard carried aloft by protestors were a great deal more complicated than most people realised.

"Decisions on which people's lives depend are not made with slogans," he said. "Real work, not the public drama surrounding it, wins the war."

Like the ousted defence minister, the commander-in-chief avoided directly personal attacks, but the object of much of his criticism was pretty clear.

"Presentations should not turn into self-representations," he wrote, in a thinly veiled swipe at Federov's long, detailed press conference last week.

"Behind each slide there should be a document, a budget and a procedure," the general went on. "Otherwise, instead of a decision the army gets disappointment."

Syrskyi's problem however is that most Ukrainians, especially the younger ones, seem to trust Federov's analysis of what needs to change in order for Ukraine to win the war, not the general's.

More than anyone, Federov is seen by many as driving innovation in the military, gradually improving Ukraine's chances of beating back its numerically superior enemy.

"Right now, the war is changing, so we need new ideas and a new structure," 44-year-old law company employee Tetiana Makarenko told me at one of the protests over the weekend.

"It's often said that a small Soviet army can't defeat a big Soviet army. It's time to change the mindset and start the new type of war."

Federov's six months at the helm in the defence ministry have coincided with a string of military successes for which the minister, not always fairly perhaps, is credited.

"We had this feeling because we saw the changing situation," Makarenko said. "We had the feeling that we can do this."

In his article, Syrskyi hit back at those accusing him of a lack of strategy, or that he had failed to embrace new technology.

"The strangest reproach that I have heard is that I supposedly do not want to fight with drones," he said, pointing out that he had presided over the creation of an unmanned systems force as a separate branch of the military.

But drones, despite being responsible for the lion's share of Russian casualties, were not enough, he argued.

"I cannot transform a million-strong army to drones in two months and say: now we fight like this."

Syrskyi said he was not at war with the defence ministry but with Russia. But while he mounts his defence against a host of critics, speculation has fallen on who Zelensky might choose to replace him.

The candidates include Mykhailo Drapatyi, commander of the Joint Forces, Andriy Bilekskyi, commander of the Third Army Corps, Oleh Apostol, commander of the Airborne Assault Forces and Ihor Obolenskyi, commander of the National Guard Corps.

All have distinguished military records, but crucially all are from a much younger generation of officers. At 46, Biletskyi is the oldest while Apostol and Obolenskyi are both 39.

At just 35, the youngest possible candidate is Brig Gen Denys Prokopenko, another National guard commander, whose "Azov" Regiment, while politically controversial for its alleged far-right connections, achieved near mythic status during the bitter siege of the southern port city of Mariupol in 2022.

Held as a prisoner of war by Russia following the collapse of the city, Prokopenko returned to Ukraine as part of a prisoner exchange in July 2023, quickly resuming his command despite Russian demands that he remain outside the country until the war was over.

Zelensky says he is still working out "what adjustments need to be made to Ukraine's defence strategy" and has thanked everyone "for their openness, honesty and substantive contributions".

With Russia expected to resume its attacks on Ukraine's energy infrastructure this autumn, the president says "Ukraine must enter August as strong as possible".

As the protests fall silent for now, the country waits for the president to make a series of possibly fateful decisions.

"If, by Friday July 24th [commander-in-chief Oleksandr] Syrskyi has...

BreakingNewsTab.title: US launches fresh strikes on Iran, as Trump warns of retaliation for deaths of soldiers
BreakingNewsTab.text:
The US military says it launched another round of strikes against Iran to "further degrade" its capabilities to attack ships in the Strait of Hormuz.

The 10th consecutive night of strikes took place after US President Donald Trump vowed to punish Iran for the deaths of US soldiers.

In response, Iran said it struck two oil tankers in the waterway, as well as US military assets in Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan, state news agency IRNA reported.

President Masoud Pezeshkian said Iran was now fighting a "full-scale war" with the US. Trump earlier said Iran had been hit "very hard" the previous night "in honour" of three US soldiers killed recently - two in Jordan and one in Iraq.

US Central Command (Centcom) said its latest strikes had hit Iranian "military command centres, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites, and air defence systems".

Explosions were reported near the north-western city of Tabriz, where state media said one person was killed and several wounded, as well at the Gulf ports of Bandar Mahshahr and Bandar Imam Khomeini and the south-eastern ports of Chabahar and Konarak.

Centcom also insisted that commercial vessels were continuing to pass through the Strait of Hormuz.

Iran meanwhile said its forces struck two oil tankers as they attempted to transit through what it called the strait's "unsafe and hazardous southern route" and rescue teams were picking up their crews, according to state media.

The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations agency (UKMTO) said a tanker in the Strait of Hormuz had reported being struck by an unknown projectile, forcing its crew to abandon the vessel and board a lifeboat.

It was not clear whether the tanker reported by UKMTO was among those cited by Iran.

Six fuel tankers and cargo ships crossed the Strait of Hormuz on Monday, according to preliminary data from maritime intelligence firm Kpler. Two of these vessels had previously been sanctioned by the US for links with Iran.

This is down from eight vessels crossing on 19 July, the data shows. All but one of the crossings made on 20 July were via the northern route near Iran.

Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) also claimed to have targeted a data centre belonging to the US company Amazon in Bahrain, according to state media on Tuesday.

The IRGC "attacked and destroyed the central data infrastructure of the American company Amazon in Bahrain with several cruise missiles," state news agency IRNA reported.

Jordan said on Tuesday that it had intercepted five drones after saying it had shot down three missiles late on Monday. Bahrain said drones launched by Iran had targeted its air navigation systems - but traffic remained unaffected. Kuwait's armed forces also said they had intercepted Iranian drones.

Later on Tuesday Kuwait said Iran had hit several power and water desalination plants the previous day.

At the same time, Tehran said diplomatic messages were still being exchanged by both sides through intermediaries, despite a collapse of a ceasefire.

On Monday night the Department of Defense confirmed the identities of two of the US soldiers – Pte Isabella Gonzales, 19, from Texas, and Lt Tyler James Feehan, 25, from Hawaii - who were killed in an Iranian strike on the Muwaffaq Salti base in Jordan over the weekend.

Centcom said earlier a third service member also went missing in action after the attack, and unidentified remains were later found in the area.
BreakingNewsTab.description: The US military says it launched another round of strikes against Iran to "further degrade" its capabilities to attack ships in the Strait of Hormuz. The 10th consecutive night of strikes took place after US President Donald Trump vowed to punish Iran for the deaths of US soldiers. In response, Iran said it struck...
BreakingNewsTab.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

BreakingNewsTab.title: Parts of the UK are on the brink of drought
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Parts of southern, central and eastern England are now close to drought, according to the National Drought Group, external.

As of Monday, East Anglia, the West Midlands, Devon, Cornwall and the Isles of Scilly, Lincolnshire and Northamptonshire, Hertfordshire and London, Wessex, Thames, Solent and the South Downs, are in "prolonged dry weather", the stage before drought.

The group, which brings together the Met Office, the National Farmers' Union, water companies and other expert bodies, made the announcement on Tuesday.

It comes as a further 10.1 million people are to be impacted by a hosepipe ban, as Thames Water announces a hosepipe ban for all its customers from Thursday.

The latest announcement means that more than 26 million people will be living under hosepipe bans by the end of this week. Agriculture and wildlife are also experiencing consequences.

Drought - simply defined as a lack of water - has no official threshold criteria, but the National Drought Group will assess conditions to decide if one should be declared.

This assessment will consider lack of rainfall, impacts on agriculture and wildlife, the likelihood of wildfires, and low reserves of water and river levels.

As well as a lack of rainfall, a contributory factor is high temperatures which evaporate water from soil. It can be influenced, too, by human activity such as deforestation, intensive farming and excessive consumption.

According to the Met Office, external, unlike most other extreme weather, drought tends to build up over time and can last from as little as a few weeks to several years.

There is pressure on public water supplies with demand increasing by up to 30% on the hottest days this July. Seven water companies have implemented or announced future hosepipe bans to reduce the strain on treatment works and storage during peak demand.

A hosepipe can use a thousand litres of water an hour, more than a family of four will use in a day.

Farmers have had to begin their harvests early and there have been warnings that cereal yields may be affected by the lack of rain.

Farmers and growers are "working around the clock to harvest crops which have come early, keep fruit and vegetables watered, and care for their livestock", according to Paul Tompkins, deputy president of the National Farmers' Union.

"Parts of the country, particularly in the east and south, have seen exceptionally low rainfall this spring and summer," he said, which could mean, "another difficult and expensive harvest after extreme weather impacts in both 2024 and 2025".

River flows have decreased in recent weeks in England according to the Environment Agency, external, with 87% of catchments now at "below normal to exceptionally low levels".

The Rivers Avon in Hampshire, Cam, Derwent in Yorkshire, Swale, Tone, Wye and Yare are exceptionally low.

National reservoir storage is 5% lower than the long-term average. Groundwater levels are seeing a usual seasonal decline but there are notably low levels in the Cotswolds and Wessex Downs.

According to the National Drought Group, by 2055 England will need to find an additional 5 billion litres of water a day to meet demand for public water supply. Water companies are investing in ten new reservoirs and exploring desalination which involves treating seawater.

Summer so far has been exceptionally hot and dry across much of the UK, with three widespread heatwaves and the longest run of dry days in central England since 1995. England, Wales and eastern Scotland have been the driest areas over recent weeks, and this follows a very dry spring in similar regions.

England has received only 3% of its long-term average July rainfall with the south and east of England seeing no rain at all. Wisley in Surrey has seen 33 consecutive dry days.

Rainfall so far this summer has been below average across the UK as a whole, but there has been large regional contrast.

Up to the middle of summer - 15 July - parts of eastern and north-eastern England had received just 26% of their total expected summer rainfall. Areas of northern and western Scotland had more than average for the first half of the summer, though.

In Wales, where just 5% of July rainfall has fallen, Natural Resources Wales has moved the worst affected regions in the north and south-east into "prolonged dry weather status", the first step in its drought plan.

Comparisons are being made with last summer, when drought was declared across much of England, Wales and eastern Scotland. It officially ended just four months ago.

This year, drought has not developed as quickly because heavy rainfall recharged our aquifers, rivers and reservoirs during a very wet autumn and winter.

However, the UK Centre for Ecology and Hydrology says flash droughts, external with a sudden transition between wet and dry conditions are "likely to happen more often, and be more intense".

As the effects of human-induced climate change continue, the Met Office expects our summers to become hotter and drier, external in the future. In fact, by 2070, our summers could be up to 60% drier and 1-6C warmer compared to 1990.

High pressure stays in charge for the rest of this week, meaning no significant rainfall for at least the next five days. We could see a few midweek showers especially near coasts in south-east England and East Anglia, as well as northern Scotland.

Through the weekend, rain-bearing weather fronts will try to move in from the north-west, but most of this rain is likely to fall across parts of Northern Ireland and Scotland. As the fronts push south, they may fizzle out, meaning little or no rainfall for the places that need it most, in the south and east.

As for the rest of summer, it is too far off to know for sure, but there are hints that things could stay mainly dry and warm, especially in the south, although summer thunderstorms are possible.

Keep up to date with the latest forecast on the BBC Weather website or app.
BreakingNewsTab.description: Parts of southern, central and eastern England are now close to drought, according to the National Drought Group, external. As of Monday, East Anglia, the West Midlands, Devon, Cornwall and the Isles of Scilly, Lincolnshire and Northamptonshire, Hertfordshire and London, Wessex, Thames, Solent and the South Downs,...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Thames Water announces hosepipe ban for 10.1m customers
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Thames Water has announced a hosepipe ban affecting 10.1 million customers after an "exceptionally warm and dry spring".

The restrictions mean, from Thursday, hosepipes should not be used for watering gardens or allotments, cleaning cars, filling paddling pools or swimming pools, or cleaning windows.

The water company said its region had seen 40% of average rainfall, and more days at or above 34C this year than ever recorded before.

Nevil Muncaster, director of strategic water resources, said: "We would not be taking this step unless it was necessary to protect water supplies for everyone and the environment."

The ban applies to all customers that Thames Water supplies with drinking water and comes into effect on Thursday.

The company provides water to customers across parts of Gloucestershire, Wiltshire, Surrey, the Thames Valley, Greater London, and the surrounding areas, external.

It provides water and wastewater services to 16 million customers overall.

It said water use was currently 10% above normal in the Thames Valley and Home Counties and 7% above normal in London, which amounted to an extra 100 million litres of water every day.

The company said this year's hot weather had meant during peak periods "water has been used faster than it can be treated and moved through parts of the network, placing additional pressure on local supplies".

"Customers should continue to use water for essential needs such as drinking, cooking, washing and hygiene," it said.

"Buckets and watering cans can still be used, and there are exemptions, including where water use is necessary for health and safety reasons or for customers with severe mobility problems."

Thames Water is about £20bn in debt.

The government recently rejected a rescue proposal, and the BBC understands the company's main lenders are preparing a legal challenge in case the new Andy Burnham-led government takes the firm into public hands.

BreakingNewsTab.title: Russian warship carried out firing exercise off Plymouth coast
BreakingNewsTab.text:
A Russian warship, shadowed by a Royal Navy vessel, carried out a live-fire artillery exercise just 46 miles (74km) south of Plymouth on Monday.

The Royal Navy monitored the exercise and is continuing to "track the vessel's activity closely", a Ministry of Defence (MoD) spokesperson said.

The Russian frigate, Neustrashimy (English: Dauntless), was also being monitored by a French military aircraft which made radio contact with the warship asking it to clarify its intentions, the BBC has been told.

The BBC was told that, prior to opening fire, the Russian warship had informed the Royal Navy Offshore Patrol Vessel, HMS Tyne, of its intention to conduct gunnery activity.

The Russian warship requested that HMS Tyne reposition to a safer distance, which it did. The Russian live-fire exercise took place in international waters and lasted for 30 minutes.

The incident will be a reminder for the new prime minister of increased Russian naval activity near UK waters in recent months. UK defence sources would not comment on whether the incident might have been timed to coincide with the start of Andy Burnham's premiership.

Last month, Royal Marine Commandos boarded a Russian "shadow fleet" oil tanker in the English Channel in a six-hour operation which was the first of its kind by UK armed forces.

Also in June, a British couple in international waters in the English Channel came across a Russian warship - Admiral Grigorovich - 23 miles off the Isle of Wight.

The frigate fired warning shots as Jane and Alan Kelvey sailed their yacht on what Russia's defence ministry said had been a "dangerous approach". However, the couple said they had not been on a "collision course".

Russian warships regularly pass through international waters in the Channel, which are separate from UK and French territorial waters.

BreakingNewsTab.title: Ryanair broken window may have been caused by 'foreign object'
BreakingNewsTab.text:
An incident on a Ryanair flight which saw a passenger partially sucked out of a plane window may have been caused by "foreign object damage" to an engine, said the airline's boss.

Michael O'Leary said an initial investigation into the incident on 10 July between Greece and Germany suggests "it looks like a foreign object damage to the engine on takeoff at Thessaloniki but ... we can't say that definitively".

He insisted that the engine on the 18-year-old Boeing 737 plane had been fully serviced and overhauled in the last two years.

Serbian national Ljubisa Karović's head and right shoulder were sucked out of the plane's window, leaving him "seriously injured and in shock".

His wife Svetlana Grković Maksimović told BBC Serbia that she and two other passengers held on to his legs for several minutes.

She said: "We pulled him back together. His entire face was deformed and blood was pouring from his nose and mouth."

He was hospitalised after the plane made an emergency landing.

The aircraft was operated by Ryanair's subsidiary Malta Air.

Confirmation that the damage was caused by an external object could affect Ryanair or Boeing's liabilities over the incident.

The US National Transportation Safety Board is investigating. O'Leary said that a draft report would be issued in about 28 days.

Passengers told local media they heard "some kind of explosion" on board.

Michael O'Leary said an initial investigation into the incident on 10 July between Greece and Germany suggests "it looks like a foreign...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Trump slaps 50% tariffs on Canada and Carney vows to 'intensify' trade talks
BreakingNewsTab.text:
US President Donald Trump has imposed a 50% tariff on a wide range of goods imported from Canada, in retaliation for what he called "unequal treatment" of US cars, dairy and alcohol.

Everyday consumer items like wine and hockey sticks and industrial goods such as cement are among the goods targeted. However, several key exports will be spared, such as energy, potash, critical minerals and fish.

Prime Minister Mark Carney responded by saying Canada stood ready to "intensify" trade talks with the US in the coming weeks.

The White House said the duties would take effect in 30 days. It marks a major escalation in trade tensions between the North American neighbours.

Trade tensions have been simmering since Trump returned to office in January 2025 and unleashed a wide-ranging global programme of tariffs, sometimes to pursue objectives not directly linked to trade.

Tariffs are taxes on imported goods that are paid to the government by companies bringing in the foreign products.

The US Supreme Court ruled earlier this year that many of Trump's tariffs imposed globally under emergency powers were illegally enacted.

But Trump has recently sought other legal avenues through which to enact his agenda, and his latest action on Monday night uses a different, obscure law that is untested in court.

Canada, which is one of the US's closest trading partners, was one of the few countries to retaliate last year against Trump's tariffs.

It placed a 25% levy of its own on about C$30bn (£16bn; $21.7bn) worth of US goods being brought into Canada. Carney later dropped some of them.

A White House fact sheet published on Monday discussing the new tariffs said they applied regardless of whether the product was included under the existing free trade agreement between Canada, the US and Mexico, known as the USMCA.

The new tariffs add to trade barriers already in place between the two nations.

The US has been maintaining active tariffs ranging from 15% to 50% on Canadian steel, aluminium and copper. It also charges a 35% tariff on Canadian softwood lumber, alongside a 25% tax on non-US parts in cars.

Meanwhile Canada has its own 25% counter-tariff on selected imports of American steel, aluminium and vehicles.

Monday's announcement also comes in the wake of President Trump's threat to impose tariffs over Canadian wildfire smoke drifting into US cities, though this issue has not been officially mentioned as the justification for the move.

As part of his executive action announcing the new tariffs, Trump issued three proclamations.

These list US complaints over trade issues that were previously known to Canada - signalling a breakdown of trade negotiations between the two neighbours. They relate to cars, dairy products and alcohol.

On cars, Trump's proclamation pointed to Canada charging a tax on imports of US motor vehicles and parts that are not covered under USMCA.

He argues it is "unreasonable" and that Canada has discriminated against the US by not charging other countries a similar tax.

Automotive manufacturing in North America is highly integrated between Canada, the US and Mexico.

Dairy, meanwhile, has long been a problem for the US, specifically Canada's supply management system, which sets limits on foreign imports. Those that exceed the limit are charged a tariff up to 300%.

And lastly, the enduring boycott of US alcoholic drinks by most Canadian provinces has become a major sore point for the Americans since it was imposed last year.

Canadian premiers have said repeatedly that the boycott will be lifted if the US removes its tariffs on key Canadian sectors, including metals and automobiles.

Canadian trade negotiators have been trying to secure a deal that would at least reduce some of the current US tariffs.

The tariffs announced on Monday, which are due to hit Canada, are due to be introduced under a different piece of legislation: Section 338 of the 1930 Tariff Act. The act covers trade discrimination rather than national emergencies.

Michael Devereux, a professor of economics at the University of British Columbia, said the move highlighted the Trump administration's opposition to USMCA.

He told BBC News: "It is a significant escalation because it directly targets goods that were previously exempt under the US, Canada, Mexico trade agreement that President Trump negotiated and signed himself in 2018."

Devereux was doubtful that it was a negotiating tactic on the part of the US leader. "I would rather see this as just an impulsive move that came from kind of a grudge that the US government and President Trump has against Canada."

Other analysts were hopeful the move might prompt new negotiations.

Candace Laing, head of the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, urged officials to make "meaningful progress" in talks before the new duties took effect in 30 days' time.

Chris Swonger, head of the Distilled Spirits Council of the United States, similarly called for both sides to find a solution, warning the decision "raises the risk of further retaliation".
BreakingNewsTab.description: US President Donald Trump has imposed a 50% tariff on a wide range of goods imported from Canada, in retaliation for what he called "unequal treatment" of US cars, dairy and alcohol. Everyday consumer items like wine and hockey sticks and industrial goods such as cement are among the goods targeted. However,...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Starmer ally takes swipe at Burnham's first big policy
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Whenever there is a reshuffle at the top of government, it is worth keeping an eye and an ear on the dislodged, demoted and rejected former ministers. What do they choose to do and say next?

Andy Burnham has started his first full day in the job with a promise to cut VAT from household electricity bills from 1 October.

But already – within hours - he is taking flak from a man who just yesterday was in a senior job in Downing Street working alongside Sir Keir Starmer.

On Monday night, the former Chief Secretary to the Prime Minister, Darren Jones, posted a picture on X of him having a beer, external, captioned "thank you and good night. See you September".

If that was meant to suggest a vow of silence it only lasted a little over 12 hours, because at breakfast time this morning came another post, this one unaccompanied by any picture but loaded with political bite.

"Good news that VAT will be cut on electricity bills," wrote Jones.

"It's a simple way for families to save a few quid, and to mechanically help to keep inflation that little bit lower. But the DigitalID program was unfunded. The government will have to set out how it will pay for its new policies at the budget."

Ouch - that will sting in Downing Street.

And yes, there are four references in the Office for Budget Responsibility's Economic and Fiscal Outlook from November, external of last year that refer to Digital ID and how and where the money will come from to pay for it is not clear.

For example, on page 10, it says "the cost of digital ID cards, for which no specific funding has been identified".

On Page 172 it talks about "the risks around the implementation of digital ID cards for which no explicit provision has been made for its £0.6bn annual cost".

Clearly, this was something of a critique of the funding of the policy Jones was previously an advocate of, so the charge was as relevant to him and Sir Keir Starmer's government about their idea as it is to Andy Burnham and his new idea.

But it is Burnham who is now prime minister and has to set out, as he put it in his first speech in Downing Street, "how we pay for" the policies they are advocating.

And all this just days after Sir Keir Starmer's successor emphasised the need to end "factionalism" within the Labour Party.

At lunchtime, Burnham will gather his cabinet for the first time, after its appointment yesterday became something of a nocturnal affair.

At gone 10pm on Monday night, there was still a minibus full of ministerial wannabes inside Downing Street waiting for their new positions to be confirmed.

One of the later posts to be filled was that of defence secretary, a role taken by Wes Streeting, whose resignation as health secretary two months ago contributed to the draining of Sir Keir's authority as prime minister.

One of the key stories of the reshuffle was a pretty significant cull of the ministers closest too and most loyal to the now former prime minister, Darren Jones among them, but also David Lammy, Peter Kyle, Lord Hermer, Steve Reed and Rachel Reeves – albeit Reeves, I am told, was offered a cabinet post but turned it down.

The question, in the coming days, weeks and months is how much do we hear in public from those banished Starmerites, as Andy Burnham's government sets out its stall.

I hadn't anticipated it starting so soon.

What do they choose to do and say next? Andy Burnham has started his first full day in the job with a promise to cut VAT from household electricity bills from 1...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Italy protests: Dozens hurt in Bologna after man dies while being restrained by police
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Protests have taken place in Italy after the death of a Moroccan-born man while being forcibly restrained by two police officers in the northern city of Bologna.

Several thousand people took to the streets on Monday night demanding "justice for Abderrahim Fakir".

A smaller group then splintered off and clashed with police into the night. Prosecutors say 64 police were hurt in the unrest.

Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni said it was imperative that the truth surrounding his death should be investigated but "nothing can justify violence against law enforcement".

Police had been called on Sunday morning after a neighbour witnessed Fakir "behaving erratically" and kicking garage doors in the working class neighbourhood of Pilastro. The officers tried to calm the situation but then used pepper spray.

A video circulating on Italian media shows two policemen sitting on Fakir as he lies face down and cries out for them to stop. A third person who is not a policeman pins his legs down as emergency workers stand by.

About two minutes into the video, Fakir stops struggling. The police officers tie his wrists and ankles and, when one of them realises Fakir is no longer moving, slaps him twice on the cheek. By the time an ambulance worker approaches, it is clear Fakir is unconscious.

Prosecutors in Bologna have opened an investigation into manslaughter against the police officers and the medical workers who were present at the scene.

Several eyewitnesses told Italian media that while Fakir appeared to be in distress he did not threaten anybody and was clearly unarmed.

"You can't die like this," his sister Khadija told Italian media. "They killed him mercilessly, and I want the truth."

Bologna mayor Matteo Lepore told Italy's La Repubblica newspaper that anyone who watched the video was "shocked".

He called for the demonstration in central Bologna on Monday evening and invited Fakir's niece to join him on stage.

"He was my uncle. He wasn't a number, he wasn't a news line... he was loved," Yossra Fakir said. "He deserved to be helped and heard, and he deserved dignity."

While the main demonstration on Monday night was peaceful, about 100 protesters clashed with police into the night and set two cars alight.

Lepore said on Tuesday he had seen "two different squares" in Bologna - "thousands of people gathered peacefully and spontaneously to express their support for Abderrahim Fakir's family" and a smaller group "who think they can respond to violence with violence".

Domenico Pianese, the Secretary-General of the police union, criticised Lepore's decision to call for a demonstration that later descended into chaos.

Calling for a protest "the day after a dramatic event like this one," Pianese said, was like calling "all the pseudo-anarchists and those who are anti-police... to take advantage and protest against the state".

Asked about what police got wrong when they detained Fakir, he said: "Judging from the images we saw, the intervention was carried out correctly."

Pianese argued that the fact the two officers had to use pepper spray and needed the help of a civilian to restrain Fakir showed that the use of force had not been excessive. "The first thing they did [after the incident] was... hand all [bodycam] footage to prosecutors," he said.
BreakingNewsTab.description: Protests have taken place in Italy after the death of a Moroccan-born man while being forcibly restrained by two police officers in the northern city of Bologna. Several thousand people took to the streets on Monday night demanding "justice for Abderrahim Fakir". A smaller group then splintered off and clashed with...
